title: Introduction
text:
Introduction

Hi! I’m Norman, an accelerated Master’s student in Data Science & Analytics with a bachelor’s degree in International Economics from Georgetown University. I have a strong focus on empirical analysis, antitrust and competition, and industrial organization. I have hands-on experience in economic consulting, where I’ve worked on cases involving price-fixing, cartels, consumer utilities, and damages analysis using large-scale datasets under leading economic experts.
This site showcases projects that reflect my interests in applied economics, econometrics, and real-world policy questions.
Use the Projects tab above to explore my work.


Relevant Consulting Experience

Economic & Statistics Consulting Intern — McClave & Associates
June 2024 – August 2024
Practice Areas: Agriculture, Retail, E-commerce, Healthcare
Reference: Jodie Newman


Incoming Analyst — Charles River Associates
Practice: Antitrust & Competition
Reference: Steve Kletter


Academic Reference — Industrial Organization & Emperical Application of Political Economy
Reference: Marius Schwartz
Reference: Juan Felipe Riaño



Technical Qualifications

SAS Studio Certified Associate
Proficient in advanced STATA, NumPy, Pandas, Sckitlearn & R studio



Favourite Reads & Studies
Here are some studies I find particularly interesting/relevant:

Dell, Lane, and Querubin (2018): Shows how historical village governance in Northern Vietnam created long-lasting local cooperation that boosted economic development compared to Southern Vietnam with a different form of historical rule.
Acemoglu, Robinson, and Santos (2013): Examines how paramilitary groups in Colombia influenced elections and persisted when they delivered votes to sympathetic politicians, challenging Max Weber’s definition of state.
Fergusson, Larreguy, and Riaño (2020): Finds that clientelistic political competition in Mexico can block investments in state capacity, using a national shock and local policy variation.
Colonnelli, Prem, and Teso (2020): Demonstrates that public service applicants’ political support strongly influence hiring in Brazil’s public sector, often prioritizing loyalty over competence.
Wantchekon (n.d.): Uses a field experiment in Benin to show how clientelism affects voting behaviour and support, finding ways to promote faireness.
Bouton et al. (2021): Analyses 340M U.S. campaign contributions to show small donors differ from large donors and respond more to candidate prominence and media.
Bertrand et al. (2020): Shows that corporate philanthropy can be strategically used as a tool for political influence, sometimes larger than PAC contributions.
Beraja et al. (2021): Finds that AI innovation in China can be mutually reinforcing with autocratic control, benefiting both technology development and regime stability.
Chen and Schwartz (2015): Shows that cost-based differential pricing often improves consumer and total welfare compared to uniform pricing or demand-based discrimination as service provision might increase.

Athletics: Georgetown University Rugby Football Club (Forward); Skiing (Extreme Terrain Qualified)
Music: Tenor Sax (School Jazz Combo); Piano (Royal Conservatory of Music, Level 8)
Games: Counter-Strike 2 (FACEIT Level 8, North America); Rainbow Six Siege (Champion, North America)






References

Acemoglu, Daron, James A. Robinson, and Rafael J. Santos. 2013. “The Monopoly of Violence: Evidence from Colombia.” Journal of the European Economic Association 11 (s1): 5–44. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1542-4774.2012.01099.x.


Beraja, Martin, Andrew Kao, David Y. Yang, and Noam Yuchtman. 2021. “AI-Tocracy.” Working {Paper}. Working Paper Series. National Bureau of Economic Research. https://doi.org/10.3386/w29466.


Bertrand, Marianne, Matilde Bombardini, Raymond Fisman, and Francesco Trebbi. 2020. “Tax-Exempt Lobbying: Corporate Philanthropy as a Tool for Political Influence.” American Economic Review 110 (7): 2065–2102. https://doi.org/10.1257/aer.20180615.


Bouton, Laurent, Julia Cage, Edgard Dewitte, and Vincent Pons. 2021. “Small Campaign Donors *.” {SSRN} {Scholarly} {Paper}. Rochester, NY: Social Science Research Network. https://doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.3978318.


Chen, Yongmin, and Marius Schwartz. 2015. “Differential Pricing When Costs Differ: A Welfare Analysis.” The RAND Journal of Economics 46 (2): 442–60. https://www.jstor.org/stable/43895598.


Colonnelli, Emanuele, Mounu Prem, and Edoardo Teso. 2020. “Patronage and Selection in Public Sector Organizations.” American Economic Review 110 (10): 3071–99. https://doi.org/10.1257/aer.20181491.


Dell, Melissa, Nathan Lane, and Pablo Querubin. 2018. “The Historical State, Local Collective Action, and Economic Development in Vietnam.” Econometrica 86 (6): 2083–2121. https://doi.org/10.3982/ECTA15122.


Fergusson, Leopoldo, Horacio Larreguy, and Juan Riaño. 2020. “Political Competition and State Capacity Evidence from a Land Allocation Program in Mexico.” {SSRN} {Scholarly} {Paper}. Rochester, NY: Social Science Research Network. https://doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.3613175.


Wantchekon. n.d. “Clientelism and Voting Behavior: Evidence from a Field Experiment in Benin  Institution for Social and Policy Studies.” Accessed April 28, 2025. https://isps.yale.edu/research/publications/isps03-05.
